options.text: Malware that encrypts files and demands payment to restore them
A type of social engineering attack via phone

questions.explanation: Ransomware encrypts the victim's files, making them inaccessible. The attacker demands a ransom payment in exchange for the decryption key.

questions.text: What is the key difference between a virus and a worm?

options.text: A virus is more harmful than a worm
A worm requires user action to spread; a virus does not
A virus requires a host file to spread; a worm spreads automatically
A worm only affects mobile devices

questions.explanation: A virus attaches to a host file and spreads when that file is run (requires user action). A worm is self-replicating and spreads across networks automatically.

questions.text: A user receives an email appearing to be from their bank asking them to click a link and enter their password. What type of attack is this?
questions.explanation: Phishing uses fraudulent emails or messages that appear to come from trusted sources to trick victims into revealing credentials or clicking malicious links.

options.text: Flooding a server with traffic from many sources to make it unavailable
Injecting SQL code into a web form

questions.explanation: DDoS (Distributed Denial of Service) uses a botnet to flood a target server with traffic, overwhelming it so legitimate users cannot access the service.

questions.text: Which protective measure requires users to verify identity using two different methods?
questions.explanation: MFA requires at least two verification factors — e.g. a password (something you know) plus a phone code (something you have) — making accounts much harder to compromise.

options.text: Encrypts all data stored on a computer
Monitors and filters network traffic based on security rules
Detects and removes malware from downloaded files

questions.explanation: A firewall monitors incoming and outgoing network traffic and blocks connections that don't match its security rules, preventing unauthorised access.

questions.text: An attacker hides malware inside a free game download. What type of attack is this?
questions.explanation: A Trojan disguises itself as legitimate or desirable software. The user willingly installs it, unknowingly giving the malware access to their system.

options.text: An attack that inserts malicious SQL code into web form inputs
A method of encrypting database records
A way to speed up database queries

questions.explanation: SQL injection inserts malicious SQL statements into input fields. If the application doesn't sanitise inputs, the injected code can manipulate or expose the database.

questions.text: Which UK law makes it illegal to access computer systems without authorisation?
questions.explanation: The Computer Misuse Act 1990 makes it illegal to access computer systems without authorisation, to modify computer material without authorisation, and to create/distribute malware.

options.text: Testing how fast a network can transfer data
Authorised ethical hacking to find security vulnerabilities
Testing whether backups can be restored

questions.explanation: Penetration testing involves authorised security professionals attempting to breach a system using real attack techniques, in order to identify and fix vulnerabilities before malicious hackers exploit them.

questions.text: What does encryption do to data?

options.text: Deletes data that is not needed
Compresses data to reduce file size
Converts data into an unreadable format that only authorised parties can decrypt
Creates a backup copy of data

questions.explanation: Encryption converts plaintext into ciphertext using an algorithm and key. Only someone with the correct decryption key can read the original data.

options.text: Flooding a network with fake traffic
Using a fabricated scenario to gain a victim's trust and extract information

questions.explanation: Pretexting is a social engineering technique where an attacker invents a believable story (a pretext) — e.g. pretending to be an IT support technician — to manipulate the victim into sharing sensitive information.
